result:
# Static

The radio in my grandmother's kitchen never fully tuned to anything. It sat on the counter between the flour canister and the window that faced the backyard, and it pulled in half of a country station, the ghost of a talk show, and underneath both of those, something that might have been music from very far away. She never adjusted it. I asked her about that once, maybe when I was nine or ten, and she said she liked knowing there was more out there than she could hear clearly.

I think about that radio more than I probably should.

---

There is a particular kind of afternoon that only exists in late September, when the light comes in at an angle that makes everything look like it's already being remembered. The shadows get long too early. The air smells like something ending. I used to dread that feeling, that premature nostalgia for a day that wasn't even over yet. Now I think it might be one of the more honest things the world does. Most beauty doesn't announce itself until it's halfway out the door.

---

I have started and abandoned the same three projects for going on two years now. A shelf in my brain is full of first chapters, half-drawn maps, playlists I made for albums that don't exist. I used to believe this was a character flaw. Now I'm not sure it isn't just the way certain kinds of thinking work — circular, returning, picking up the same stone and turning it over not because you forgot what was underneath but because you want to see if anything has changed. Sometimes something has. A beetle. A change in the color of the dirt. Worth checking.

---

My friend called me last Tuesday and we talked for two hours about nothing that would survive summarizing. What her neighbor's dog did. A documentary we'd both half-watched. The way some songs feel like places you've never been. It was the best conversation I'd had in months. I don't know why we stopped calling each other for so long before that. Fear of interrupting, maybe. Everyone walks around assuming they are an interruption.

You are not an interruption.

---

I read once that trees in a forest share resources through fungal networks underground — the sick ones get fed by the healthy ones, the old stumps kept chemically alive by their neighbors for decades after they've fallen. Scientists named it, studied it, wrote papers. But I keep thinking about what it means that this was always happening, long before anyone named it. The forest didn't need the vocabulary. It just did the thing.

---

There's a version of courage that looks nothing like bravery. It looks like making the appointment you've been putting off for eight months. It looks like sending the awkward text. It looks like eating something real for dinner instead of standing over the sink with crackers, telling yourself you'll do better tomorrow. Small rescues. The kind nobody claps for. The kind that accumulate into a life that is, quietly, sustainable.

---

I want to tell you something I'm not sure I believe consistently but that I believe right now, which is that the things you carry that feel like weakness are almost never actually weakness. The oversensitivity. The replaying of conversations. The way certain music makes you have to sit down. That's not malfunction. That's the equipment working at high resolution. The problem isn't the sensitivity, it's the world that keeps calling it a problem.

---

My grandmother is gone now. The house was sold years ago. But I have this clear, specific memory of standing in that kitchen in the early morning when everyone else was asleep, listening to that radio do its best with whatever signals were passing through the air, and feeling — not happy exactly, but located. Like I knew where I was. Like that was enough.

That's what I'm chasing, I think. Not happiness as a destination but location as a feeling. The sense of: I am here, in this specific moment, in this specific light, and the radio is playing something I can't quite make out, and somewhere outside there is a whole world transmitting, and I am alive enough to receive some small part of it.

---

The late September light is doing its thing again outside my window. The shadows are long and everything looks like a photograph of itself. I'm not dreading it this time. I'm just watching it happen, trying to stay in the room with it, trying not to already be somewhere else in my head.

The static. The signal. Whatever comes through.

The static. The signal. Whatever comes through.
